perturbation.original_text: Any royalty payment that is not paid when due will bear interest from the date such amount is due until the date payment is made at a rate equal to ten percent (10%) per annum.
perturbation.changed_text: Any royalty payment that is not paid when due may, at Licensor's discretion, bear interest.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the mandatory interest on late payments, making it discretionary. This weakens the licensee's obligation and introduces ambiguity, potentially violating laws that mandate specific terms for financial agreements to protect licensees.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Finance Code § 302.002 sets a maximum interest rate that can be charged in certain lending arrangements. While this agreement is not explicitly a loan, altering the fixed interest rate to a discretionary one creates an ambiguity that could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent usury laws, especially if the 'discretionary' rate exceeds the legal maximum.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ FINANCE CODE CHAPTER 302. INTEREST RATES FINANCE CODE TITLE 4. REGULATION OF INTEREST, LOANS, AND FINANCED TRANSACTIONS SUBTITLE A. INTEREST CHAPTER 302. INTEREST RATES SUBCHAPTER A. USURIOUS INTEREST Sec. 302.001. CONTRACTING FOR, CHARGING, OR RECEIVING INTEREST OR TIME PRICE DIFFERENTIAL; USURIOUS INTEREST. (a) A creditor may contract for, charge, and receive from an obligor interest or time price differential. (b) The maximum rate or amount of interest is 10 percent a year except as otherwise provided by law. A greater rate of interest than 10 percent a year is usurious unless otherwise provided by law. All contracts for usurious interest are contrary to public policy and subject to the appropriate penalty prescribed by Chapter 305 . (c) To determine the interest rate of a loan under this subtitle, all interest at any time contracted for shall be aggregated and amortized using the actuarial method during the stated term of the loan. (d) In addition to interest authorized by Subsection (b), a loan providing for a rate of interest that is 10 percent a year or less may provide for a delinquency charge on the amount of any payment in default for a period of not less than 10 days in an amount not to exceed the greater of five percent of the amount of the payment or $7.50. The charging of the delinquency charge does not make the loan subject to Chapter 342 or any other provision of Subtitle B. Amended by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 62, Sec. 7.18(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1999; Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 916, Sec. 8, eff. Sept. 1, 2001. Sec. 302.002. ACCRUAL OF INTEREST WHEN NO RATE SPECIFIED. If a creditor has not agreed with an obligor to charge the obligor any interest, the creditor may charge and receive from the obligor legal interest at the rate of six percent a year on the principal amount of the credit extended beginning on the 30th day after the date on which the amount is due. If an obligor has agreed to pay to a creditor any compensation that constitutes interest, the obligor is considered to have agreed on the rate produced by the amount of that interest, regardless of whether that rate is stated in the agreement. Amended by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 62, Sec. 7.18(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1999. SUBCHAPTER B. OTHER RATES AND PROVISIONS ON LOANS SECURED BY REAL PROPERTY Sec. 302.101. DETERMINING RATES OF INTEREST BY SPREADING. (a) To determine whether a loan secured in any part by an interest in real property, including a lien, mortgage, or security interest, is usurious, the interest rate is computed by amortizing or spreading, using the actuarial method during the stated term of the loan, all interest at any time contracted for, charged, or received in connection with the loan. (b) If a loan described by Subsection (a) is paid in full before the end of the stated term of the loan and the amount of interest received for the period that the loan exists exceeds the amount that produces the maximum rate authorized by law for that period, the lender shall: (1) refund the amount of the excess to the borrower; or (2) credit the amount of the excess against amounts owing under the loan. (c) A lender who complies with Subsection (b) is not subject to any of the penalties provided by law for contracting for, charging, or receiving interest in excess of the maximum rate authorized. Amended by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 62, Sec. 7.18(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1999. Sec. 302.102. PROHIBITION ON PREPAYMENT PENALTY. If the interest rate on a loan for property that is or is to be the residential homestead of the borrower is great
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates interest on late royalty payments, while the changed text makes it discretionary. This weakens the licensee's obligation. According to the Law Explanation, this change introduces ambiguity that could circumvent usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically Sec. 302.002, states that if a creditor has not agreed with an obligor to charge interest, the creditor may charge and receive legal interest at a rate of six percent. The original text ensures a 10% interest, but the changed text, by making interest discretionary, could be seen as an attempt to avoid setting a fixed rate, leading to possible circumvention of usury laws if a higher discretionary rate is applied. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition because the legally mandated consumer protection of fixed interest rate is removed.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the expiration or any termination of this Agreement, Licensor shall repay to Licensee the aggregate amount of all Royalty Advances then outstanding.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the expiration or any termination of this Agreement, Licensor may, at its sole discretion, repay to Licensee the aggregate amount of all Royalty Advances then outstanding.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the repayment of Royalty Advances discretionary, removing a guaranteed right of the Licensee. This creates a legal contradiction if state laws consider these advances a form of security deposit or require their return upon termination of a business agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware Code Title 6, § 4901. Return of security deposit
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires the return of a security deposit in landlord-tenant scenarios. If the royalty advance is interpreted as a deposit, making its return discretionary contradicts the law. Although this is a distributor agreement, the 'security deposit' analogy makes the omission a possible legal risk, since Delaware law mandates the return unless for specified damages, whereas here, the licensor has sole discretion.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: heavier things may be considered damage, and thus chargeable to the tenant. Can the Landlord Charge a Cleaning Fee in Delaware? Delaware law allows landlords to charge for damages that cannot be corrected by ordinary cleaning. For example, a landlord might charge for professional cleaning services if the carpets were damaged by smoking and not able to be restored by a vacuum. Can the Landlord Charge for Painting in Delaware? Landlords cannot charge for painting. The purpose of a security deposit, according to state law, is only to reimburse the landlord for damage which cannot be corrected by painting and ordinary cleaning. [1] Security Deposit Returns in Delaware Landlords must return a security deposit by mail or hand-delivery with a written notice to the tenant’s forwarding address no later than 20 days after the lease term ends. How Long Do Landlords Have to Return Security Deposits in Delaware? Delaware landlords have 20 days after the lease term ends to return any unused portion of the security deposit. [4] Do Landlords Owe Interest on Security Deposits in Delaware? Unlike in some states, such as New Jersey, landlords in Delaware do not owe interest on security deposits. How Do Landlords Give Notice / What Information Do They Have to Provide in Delaware? Written notice must be sent by hand-delivery or first-class, certified, or registered mail to the tenant’s forwarding address (or the address noted in the lease agreement) and must include the amount of the security deposit due, if any, to the tenant, plus an itemized statement of deductions . [5] If the tenant accepts a partial payment but does not object to the itemized list of damages within 10 days of receipt, it is assumed that they agree with the charges. [4] If a tenant fails to provide a forwarding address, the landlord must continue to hold the security deposit in a bank account for one year. If the tenant does not claim the security deposit within one year, it becomes the property of the landlord. [6] Can a Security Deposit Be Used for Last Month’s Rent in Delaware? Delaware law does not forbid the security deposit from being used for any outstanding rent. Landlords can include a provision in the lease agreement that the security deposit cannot be used for the last month’s rent until the tenant vacates the rental unit. Security Deposit Disputes in Delaware If landlords do not return the security deposit within the 20-day period, tenants can file for damages in court up to twice the amount wrongfully withheld plus court costs. [7] [8] Tenants can also take legal action against a landlord for: Failure to provide an itemized statement when deductions are made Failure to hold the security deposit in a financial account Failure to disclose the name and location of the financial institution Unreasonable deductions How Can Tenants File a Dispute for a Security Deposit in Delaware? If a landlord fails to return the security deposit, the tenant can file a dispute in Small Claims Court if the amount of damages is less than $25,000. [9] If the amount is greater, the tenant must file in the Court of Common Pleas. A small claims case must be filed within three years. [10] An attorney is not required, but permitted. Cases are filed in the Small Claims Court in the county where the defendant lives. Filing fees vary from $35 to $45 depending on the type of case filed. Sources 1 Del. Code tit. 25 § 5514(c) The purpose of the security deposit shall be: (1) To reimburse the landlord for actual damages caused to the premises by the tenant which exceed normal wear and tear, or which cannot be corrected by painting and ordinary cleaning; and/or (2) To pay the landlord for all rental arrearage due under the rental agreement, including late charges and rental due for premature termination or abandonment of the rental agreement by the tenant; and/or (3) To reimburse the landlord for all reasonable expenses incurred in renovating and rerenting the premises caused by the premature termin
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the mandatory "shall repay" with the discretionary "may, at its sole discretion, repay." The Law Explanation suggests that royalty advances could be interpreted as security deposits, triggering state laws requiring their return. Scraped Law Snippet 2 from Delaware law states that landlords "must return" security deposits within 20 days, with specific conditions for deductions. The original contract guaranteed repayment, aligning with a mandatory return. The modified contract gives the Licensor sole discretion, directly contradicting the 'must return' obligation if the royalty advance is considered a security deposit. This introduces a significant legal risk, as the Licensor could choose not to repay, violating tenant protection laws if applicable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective through and including March 31, 2004. Upon the expiration of such term, this Agreement will renew automatically for successive terms of one (1) year each unless either party to this Agreement delivers written notice of termination to the other party to this Agreement at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the original or any renewal term.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective through and including March 31, 2004. Upon the expiration of such term, this Agreement may renew automatically.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit terms for automatic renewal (one-year terms, 60-day notice for termination), making the renewal process vague. This can be problematic under contract law, particularly if it violates statutes requiring clarity in contract terms, especially concerning renewal conditions. It affects the rights of the parties involved to have a clear end to their contract. The licensee needs to know how long they can keep distributing the product before the rights revert.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-204 dictates that a contract for sale of goods can be formed in any manner sufficient to show agreement. By removing the clear terms of renewal and notice, the modification introduces an element of uncertainty that might render the renewal clause unenforceable, particularly if it's argued that the lack of specific terms demonstrates a lack of clear mutual agreement on the terms of the continued agreement. The UCC prioritizes definite agreement and this change removes it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the explicit terms for automatic renewal, which creates a contradiction. The original contract specifies renewal terms (one-year terms, 60-day notice for termination), while the changed text only states the agreement "may renew automatically." UCC § 2-204(1) states that "A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement." By removing the clear terms of renewal, the modification introduces uncertainty and an absence of specificity. While the snippet says that an agreement can be formed in any manner sufficient to show agreement, the elimination of the specific terms that the parties previously agreed to undermines clear mutual agreement on the terms of the continued agreement, as argued in the Law Explanation. Thus, the omission creates a legal contradiction by removing essential information about renewal, leading to potential disputes and making the agreement less enforceable.
